content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
926 J Street, Suite 523
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

June 19, 1975

Andrew Eber
Government Relations Director
United Way of the Bay Area
2015 Steiner Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Re: Opinion Request
No. 75-131

Dear Mr. Eber:

Thank you for your letter dated June 5, 1975,
requesting an opinion with respect to your duties under
the Political Reform Act.

Since the questions you have posed raise no
substantial question involving interpretation of the
Political Reform Act, your request for a formal
opinion is denied.

You ask whether communications with state agencies
for grants, contracts, and services may constitute
lobbying. The Political Reform Act recognizes that
many actions involving administrative agencies are not
lobbying.

"Lobbying," as defined in the Act, is limited
to communications "for the purpose of influencing ...
administrative action." (Government Code Section 82039)
"Administrative action" is further defined in Government
Code Section 82002 to include rate-making and quasi-
legislative proceedings. The conduct that you describe
is not influencing rate-making or quasi-legislative pro-
ceedings.

Secondly, you ask whether people who testify at
the unsolicited request of state agencies are "lobbyists"
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. This
issue is considered in a request presently pending before
the Commission (Automobile Club of Southern California,
Opinion Request No. 75-075). The Commission invites your
comments on the issue. You should keep in mind, however,
that the question is relevant only to quasi-legislative
and rate-making proceedings.

Andrew Eber
June 19, 1975
Page 2


     I hope that the foregoing is sufficiently helpful.
If you have any further questions, I urge you to contact
a member of the Commission staff.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman, for the
                                        Commission

DHL:jp

2515 STEINER STREET • SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94115 • 563-1222

OFFICERS
C W CLAUSEN
Chairman
LAURENCE D SOLING
Vice-Chairman
AROL L MCCABLA
President
GORDON L HOUGH
Resident-Elect
VAS WALLACE D SOHNNER
Senior Vice-President
STEVEN K MARTIN
Secretary
LARRY WONG
Treasurer

COMMITTEE CHAIRMEN
AND VICE-PRESIDENTS
THAO BROWN
Agencies & Institutions
JAMES W PORTER
Legislation
JOHN F CROWLEY
F Participation
H R FINLAND JR
Public Relations
MRS WILLIAM SHEAFF
Employer Relation
JOHN W KENNEY
Campaign '75

ADMINISTRATION
ROBERT L YOUNG
Executive Director
CAMILLE S REED
Associate Executive-Director
EVA CAMPBELL-DAVIS
Agency Services Director
DENLAH KOESKI
Public Relations Director
C. ARNOLD TERVO
Director of Government Relations
HELEN SCHULTZ
Finance Administrator Director

June 5, 1975

Mr. Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
926 "J" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

Several of the 190 private non-profit agencies
which are members of the United Way of the Bay
Area have asked me to request an opinion from
the FPPC regarding their possible status as
lobbyists, in relation to two particular aspects
of their operations.

As you know, many private agencies hold a variety
of grants or other forms of authorizations to
provide human care services on behalf of the State
of California. As these agencies compete for
available grants, negotiate contracts based on
these grants, and attempt to administer the services
required by these grants, they often must compete
care with various state departments of agencies
on a regular, and occasionally intensive, basis.

Can the communications resulting from this legally
mandated competition, negotiation, and administra-
tion, be construed as an attempt to influence ad-
ministrative action? I would hope not, since most
of these agencies are attempting to provide critically
needed human services, often with a staff already
spread desperately thin and a bare bones budget.
These agencies have neither the time nor the money
to meet the lobbying registration requirements pro-
mulgated by your Commission, although the efforts
expanded in relationship to state funding, upon
which some of the agencies literally depend for
survival, may fall within some of your lobbying
definitions.

OFFICES
515 Seventeenth Street                4505 Macdonald Avenue             205 West 20th Avenue            2144 Point Street
Oakland, California 94612            Richmond, California 94805        P.O.Box 2107                    San Francisco, California 94101
331-3132                            233-1111                          San Mateo, California 94402    456-0213

Mr. D.E.Burwell
1453
Lynches L.
Page 125

Extract

Is it true that you have always been a believer and a restorationist and a universalist?

I believe in the doctrine of restitution or restoration and in the universal atonement of Christ, and I believe that all mankind will eventually be saved and restored to the image of God. But I do not believe in the endless torment of the wicked. I believe in the doctrine of restitution.

Now Mr. Burwell, you say you are a believer in the restitution doctrine. The Scriptures seem to teach that some will be lost eternally. How do you reconcile your belief in universal salvation with the plain teachings of the Word of God?

I believe the Scriptures teach that Christ died for all men, and that His atonement is sufficient for all, and that eventually all men will accept Christ and be saved. I do not believe the Bible teaches eternal damnation.

But does not the Bible speak of outer darkness, the lake of fire, the second death, and many other expressions that indicate endless punishment?

These expressions are used to indicate the nature and severity of punishment in this life and the next, but I believe they are not endless. I believe that eventually all punishment will end and all men will be restored to fellowship with God.

Do you believe the Bible is the Word of God?

Yes, I believe the Bible is the Word of God, but I believe it must be interpreted in the light of the character of God, which is Love. God is Love, and a God of Love would not eternally torment His creatures.

Mr. Burwell, you say you believe the Bible is the Word of God, but you do not accept its plain teachings regarding eternal punishment. Is this not a contradiction?

I do not think it is a contradiction. I believe the Bible teaches what I have stated, and I believe my interpretation is in harmony with the character of God and the teachings of Christ.

Mr. Daniel Lowenstein
June 5, 1975
Page 2


Also, I hope that you will send me copies of the
reporting forms and reporting procedures applicable
to your regulations.

Thank you for your cooperation and attention to
these matters.

Sincerely,

Andrew Eber
Government Relations Director

AJE:cls
